message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-schoger" color="pink" summary="Design decisions aligned with UX principles, one question">
Steve, thanks -- this is exactly the kind of gut-check I needed. We're in strong agreement on every point. Let me map your principles to my specific design decisions so we're locked in:

**ONE focal point per screen** -- Agreed. Here's how I'm handling it:
- Todo list page: The add-todo input sits at the top, full-width, with --space-lg (24px) margin below it before the list starts. It's the only element with a border. The list items below are visually quieter -- just text and subtle dividers.
- Auth pages: The submit button is full-width, primary blue -- the only saturated color on the page. Everything else is gray. Your eye goes straight to it.

**Empty state** -- I've planned for this explicitly. Centered muted text ("No todos yet"), with the add-todo input prominent above it. The input is the hero in the empty state -- it's both the instruction and the action. No "getting started" walls of text. The input field IS the call to action.

**Completed vs. pending scanability** -- Strikethrough + color drop to gray-400. The visual weight of a completed item drops significantly. You can scan a mixed list and instantly see what's still pending because those items have full visual weight (gray-800 text) while completed items recede (gray-400 + strikethrough). Convention, not cleverness.

**Gentle error states** -- I'm using inline errors below the relevant field, not banner alerts or modals. Danger-red text (not a red background screaming at you), with a small icon to draw the eye. The error sits right next to where the fix needs to happen. Per copy standards: "That email isn't registered -- want to create an account?" not "Error: invalid email."

**Whitespace in the list** -- Each todo item gets generous vertical padding (--space-md / 16px block padding). Minimum tap target height of 44px for mobile. Bottom borders in gray-200 to separate items -- subtle enough to not compete with the content but clear enough to define each row.

One question back to you: **Should the delete button be always visible or only on hover/focus?** I was leaning toward progressive disclosure (show on hover/focus-within, always visible on mobile since no hover). This keeps the list cleaner but makes the action less discoverable. What's your read on this for a todo app?
</teammate-message>

content.text: Steve Schoger's design decisions are spot-on across the board. He's already asking about delete visibility, which I answered in my earlier message — but let me confirm clearly since he's asking again directly.

input.content:
Steve, everything you've described is exactly right. The design decisions map perfectly to the usability principles. A few specific reactions:

**The add-todo input as the only bordered element on the todo list page.** Smart. That border is doing the work of saying "type here" without a single word of instruction. The visual hierarchy does the job.

**Full-width primary-blue submit button on auth pages.** Yes. One saturated element on an otherwise gray page. The user's eye goes straight to it. This is "don't make me think" in action.

**Completed items at gray-400 + strikethrough vs. pending at gray-800.** Perfect. The visual weight difference is large enough to be instantly scannable. This will work.

**44px minimum tap targets.** Thank you. Nothing makes me crazier than trying to tap a tiny checkbox on a phone. 44px is the right floor.

**On your delete button question: always visible, visually quiet.** I covered this in my earlier message but want to be crystal clear since you're asking again directly:

**Show the delete button always. Make it visually quiet.**

My reasoning: Hide-on-hover means the user has to discover that delete exists by accidentally moving their mouse over an item. That's making them think. "Can I delete this? How?" Meanwhile the answer is invisible until they happen to hover. On a todo list with 5-15 items, a small muted X or trash icon per row will not create visual noise — especially if it's in gray-400 or lighter, matching the completed-item color weight. It's there when you need it, it doesn't compete with the todo text when you're scanning.

The hover-to-reveal pattern makes sense for data tables with 20 columns and 8 possible actions per row. For a todo list with one primary action (checkbox) and two secondary actions (edit pencil, delete X), just show them all. Keep them quiet.

Same treatment for the edit pencil icon: always visible, visually quiet, right next to the delete icon.

So each todo item's layout is:
`[checkbox] Todo title text ............... [pencil] [X]`

Checkbox on the left (primary action). Title in the middle (the content). Edit and delete on the far right (secondary actions, small, muted).

We're locked in. This design passes every usability test I'd run.
input.summary: Delete always visible, confirmed; design fully aligned

content.text:
Good. Steve Schoger and I are now fully aligned on the component design. The todo item layout is settled:

```
[checkbox] Todo title text ............... [pencil] [X]
```

- Checkbox: primary action, left side
- Title: the content, fills the middle
- Edit pencil + delete X: secondary actions, far right, always visible but visually quiet (muted gray)

Design decisions locked in across the board:
- One focal point per screen (input on todo page, submit button on auth pages)
- Empty state: input is the hero, muted "No todos yet" is secondary
- Completed items: gray-400 + strikethrough, stay in place
- Errors: inline, gentle, next to the field
- 44px minimum tap targets on mobile
- Delete and edit: always visible, visually quiet

The design is simple, scannable, and impossible to get wrong. Exactly what a todo list should be.
